masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through American History
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through American History
masthead.dek: Twenty questions whose answers all sit in walkable sets - presidents, states, decades, counts. The difficulty is in the slicing, not the obscurity.
masthead.footer_html: Verified against National Archives, Library of Congress, Britannica, Miller Center and HISTORY.

questions.q: The first thirteen states were the original colonies. Which state was the very next one to join the country after them?
questions.detail: Vermont became the 14th state on March 4, 1791, the first added after the original 13.
questions.why: It was an independent republic for 14 years before joining as state number 14 in 1791.
questions.more: Kentucky followed as the 15th state in 1792; New York dropped its land claims for $30,000 to clear Vermont's path.
questions.source: Constituting America; EBSCO Research Starters (Vermont admitted to the Union, 1791)
questions._mechanism: slicer (removes the original 13) - difficulty med

questions.q: Of all the men actually voted into the presidency, who was the youngest at the time he won?
questions.detail: Kennedy was 43 years, 236 days old when he took office, the youngest ever elected president.
questions.why: He edged out a field of older nominees to become the youngest elected president at 43.
questions.more: Theodore Roosevelt was younger overall at 42, but he reached the office by succession, not election.
questions.source: JFK Library; howstuffworks (The Youngest President of the United States)
questions._mechanism: slicer (removes Theodore Roosevelt by requiring 'elected') - difficulty hard

questions.q: Which president stood taller than any other when he held the office?
questions.detail: Lincoln measured 6 feet 4 inches, the tallest U.S. president.
questions.why: At 6 feet 4 inches he towered about 9 inches over the average man of his day.
questions.more: Lyndon Johnson is second at 6 feet 3.5 inches, a full inch shorter than Lincoln.
questions.source: POTUS.com Presidential Heights; History Facts (presidents in height order)
questions._mechanism: reflex-defeater (people guess Washington) - difficulty med

questions.q: Among the states that have never been the birthplace of a president, which one has the most people living in it today?
questions.detail: Florida is the most populous state that has never produced a president by birth.
questions.why: As the third most populous state overall, it tops the list of states with no native-born president.
questions.more: Despite its size and political weight, no president has yet been born inside its borders.
questions.source: YouTube/apaitu trivia answer (most populous state never birthplace of a president)
questions._mechanism: slicer (removes Texas, California, New York which all produced presidents) - difficulty hard

questions.q: Who was president when the stock market collapsed and the Great Depression began?
questions.detail: The market crashed in October 1929, less than eight months into Hoover's term.
questions.why: The crash hit in October 1929, just months after he took office in March 1929.
questions.more: Franklin Roosevelt, often linked to the Depression, did not take office until 1933, more than three years later.
questions.source: Britannica (Stock market crash of 1929); Lumen Learning (Hoover and the Stock Market Crash)
questions._mechanism: reflex-defeater (people guess FDR) + non-decorative route (no year stated) - difficulty med

questions.q: One state ran itself as its own independent country for almost a decade before it joined the United States. Which state?
questions.detail: Texas was an internationally recognized republic from 1836 to 1845, then joined as the 28th state.
questions.why: It governed itself as the Republic of Texas for nine years before annexation in 1845.
questions.more: Texas remains the only U.S. state to have first existed as a fully recognized independent republic.
questions.source: history.state.gov (US recognition, Texas); Wikipedia (1845 annexation of Texas)
questions._mechanism: slicer (removes California, Hawaii) - difficulty med

questions.q: James Buchanan led the country in the years just before the Civil War. Who was the president directly before him?
questions.detail: Pierce was the 14th president (1853-1857); Buchanan was the 15th, taking office in 1857.
questions.why: Pierce served immediately before Buchanan, handing off in March 1857.
questions.more: Both Northern Democrats, Pierce and Buchanan are often ranked among the weakest presidents for failing to stop secession.
questions.source: Britannica (James Buchanan); Wikipedia (Franklin Pierce)
questions._mechanism: non-decorative route (must order the presidents) - difficulty hard

questions.q: Counting every man who has ever held the office, not just those elected, who was the youngest to become president?
questions.detail: Roosevelt was 42 years, 322 days old when he took over after McKinley's death in 1901.
questions.why: At 42 he became the youngest person ever to hold the office, a year younger than Kennedy.
questions.more: He rose through the vice presidency after McKinley was assassinated, rather than winning the top job outright.
questions.source: Wikipedia (List of presidents by age); constitutionus.com (Who Was the Youngest President?)
questions._mechanism: reflex-defeater (vs Kennedy) - difficulty hard

questions.q: Of the fifty states, which one was the most recent to join the country?
questions.detail: Hawaii became the 50th state on August 21, 1959.
questions.why: It joined in August 1959 as the 50th and final state.
questions.more: Alaska had become the 49th state just eight months earlier in January 1959.
questions.source: National Archives (Hawaii Statehood); HISTORY (Hawaii becomes 50th state)
questions._mechanism: reflex-defeater (people guess Alaska) - difficulty med

questions.q: Who was president when federal soldiers were sent to escort Black students into a Little Rock high school?
questions.detail: Eisenhower sent the 101st Airborne to Little Rock Central High in September 1957.
questions.why: He federalized the Arkansas National Guard and dispatched about 1,000 paratroopers in 1957.
questions.more: He acted against Governor Orval Faubus after the state tried to block the Little Rock Nine.
questions.source: HISTORY (Why Eisenhower Sent Federal Troops to Little Rock); National Archives (Executive Order 10730)
questions._mechanism: non-decorative route (no year stated) - difficulty hard

questions.q: How many states broke away to form the Confederacy?
questions.detail: Eleven Southern states seceded to form the Confederate States of America in 1860-61.
questions.why: Seven seceded first, then four more after Fort Sumter, totaling eleven.
questions.more: Border states like Kentucky and Missouri had divided loyalties but never formally joined the eleven.
questions.source: Britannica (Confederate States of America); American Battlefield Trust (Secession Acts)
questions._mechanism: reflex-defeater (people guess 13) + count - difficulty med

questions.q: Which president struck the deal that roughly doubled the size of the United States overnight?
questions.detail: Jefferson's 1803 Louisiana Purchase added about 827,000 square miles.
questions.why: His purchase of the Louisiana Territory in 1803 nearly doubled the nation's land for $15 million.
questions.more: The deal worked out to roughly five cents an acre across the new territory.
questions.source: Monticello (The Louisiana Purchase); National Archives (Louisiana Purchase Treaty, 1803)
questions._mechanism: non-decorative route (no year stated) - difficulty med

questions.q: Who was president when the United States entered the First World War?
questions.detail: Wilson led the U.S. into World War I in April 1917 after years of neutrality.
questions.why: He asked Congress for a war declaration in April 1917 over Germany's submarine warfare.
questions.more: He had won reelection in 1916 partly on having kept the country out of the war.
questions.source: Wikipedia (United States in World War I); HISTORY (Woodrow Wilson)
questions._mechanism: non-decorative route (no year stated) - difficulty hard

questions.q: Who was president when Japan attacked Pearl Harbor and pulled the country into World War II?
questions.detail: Roosevelt delivered the 'Day of Infamy' speech the day after the December 7, 1941 attack.
questions.why: He asked Congress to declare war on December 8, 1941, the day after the attack.
questions.more: It was the fourth term of office he would eventually win; he had already led the country for over eight years by then.
questions.source: National Archives (Joint Address, Declaration of War Against Japan); HISTORY (Pearl Harbor bombed)
questions._mechanism: non-decorative route (no year stated) - difficulty easy

questions.q: Who was the first president to win the White House, lose it, and then win it back, serving two terms that did not touch?
questions.detail: Cleveland served as the 22nd (1885-1889) and the 24th (1893-1897) president.
questions.why: He lost the 1888 electoral vote, then won 277 electors in 1892 to return for a separated second term.
questions.more: He won the popular vote in 1888 but lost the Electoral College to Benjamin Harrison before his comeback.
questions.source: Britannica (Grover Cleveland); Ballotpedia (Grover Cleveland)
questions._mechanism: slicer (asks 'first' to split-term, removes later cases) - difficulty hard

questions.q: About how many men signed the Declaration of Independence?
questions.detail: Fifty-six delegates signed the Declaration of Independence.
questions.why: The fifty-six signers ranged from 26-year-old Edward Rutledge to 70-year-old Benjamin Franklin.
questions.more: Most signed an engrossed copy on August 2, 1776; Thomas McKean signed last, in 1777.
questions.source: Constitution Center (About the Signers); Harvard Declaration Resources Project
questions._mechanism: count with a guessable range - difficulty med

questions.q: Every early president was born a British subject. Who was the first president actually born as a citizen of the United States?
questions.detail: Van Buren, born in 1782 in Kinderhook, New York, was the first president born after independence.
questions.why: Born in 1782, he was the first president who was never a British subject.
questions.more: He grew up speaking Dutch, making him the only president whose first language was not English.
questions.source: History Facts (Van Buren first president born in the US); Miller Center (Van Buren)
questions._mechanism: slicer (removes Washington through Jackson, all born before 1776) - difficulty hard

questions.q: Who was president when both the Civil Rights Act and the Voting Rights Act were signed into law?
questions.detail: Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act in July 1964 and the Voting Rights Act in August 1965.
questions.why: He signed both landmark laws within about thirteen months of each other in 1964 and 1965.
questions.more: The earlier movement was led under Kennedy, but Kennedy was assassinated before either bill became law.
questions.source: Wikipedia (Civil Rights Act of 1964); National Archives (Voting Rights Act, 1965)
questions._mechanism: reflex-defeater (people guess Kennedy) + non-decorative route - difficulty med

questions.q: All or part of how many of today's states were carved out of the land bought in the Louisiana Purchase?
questions.detail: The Louisiana Purchase land became all or part of 15 present-day states.
questions.why: Its 828,000 square miles eventually formed all or part of fifteen states.
questions.more: The territory stretched from the Gulf Coast up to today's Montana and the Canadian border.
questions.source: constitutioncenter.org (Louisiana Purchase completed); HISTORY (Louisiana Purchase)
questions._mechanism: count with a guessable range - difficulty hard

questions.q: Which president held the job for the shortest time, dying after only about a month in office?
questions.detail: Harrison died of pneumonia on April 4, 1841, about a month into his term.
questions.why: He served roughly 31 days, the shortest tenure of any president.
questions.more: He gave the longest inaugural address ever, nearly two hours in the cold, before falling ill.
questions.source: HISTORY (Harrison dies, April 4, 1841); Wikipedia (List of presidents by time in office)
questions._mechanism: slicer (asks shortest tenure rather than naming him) - difficulty med
